abstract.text: Arrange information into rows and columns.

inlineContent.text: To add a table to your documentation, start a new paragraph and add the following required elements, each on its own line:
A table header, with the heading text of each column, separated by pipes (
A separator row, with at least 3 hyphens (
) for each column, separated by pipes
One or more rows of table cells, separating each cell of formatted content by a pipe

code: `slow`       | Moves slightly faster than a snail
`supersonic` | Moves faster than the speed of sound

inlineContent.text: The example markup above defines the table that’s shown below. Each column is only as wide as its widest cell and the table is only as wide as the sum of its columns.
Moves slightly faster than a snail
Moves faster than the speed of sound
You don’t need to pad the cells to align the column separators (
). However, it might make your table
easier to read, especially for large or complex tables.
You can also define the same table with the markup that’s shown below. All other examples will use padded cells for readability.

code: `supersonic`|Moves faster than the speed of sound

inlineContent.text: You can add leading and/or trailing pipes (
) if you find that table markup easier to read. This doesn’t affect the rendered table on the page. The leading and trailing pipes
be applied inconsistently for each row, but doing so may make it harder to discern the structure of the table.

code: | `slow`       | Moves slightly faster than a snail   |
| `supersonic` | Moves faster than the speed of sound |

inlineContent.text: The content of a table cell supports the same style attributes as other text and supports links to other content, including symbols. If a table cell’s content includes a pipe (
) you need to escape the pipe character by preceding it with a backslash
). For more information about styling text, see

inlineContent.overridingTitle: Format Text in Bold, Italics, and Code Voice

overridingTitleInlineContent.text: Format Text in Bold, Italics, and Code Voice

inlineContent.text: A table cell can only contain a single line of formatted content. Lists, asides, code blocks, headings, and directives are not supported inside a table cell.
By default, all table columns align their content to the leading edge. To change the horizontal alignment for a column, modify the table’s separator row to add a colon (
) for that column, either before the hyphens for leading alignment, before
after the hyphens for center alignment, or after the hyphens for trailing alignment.
By default, each table cell is one column wide and one row tall. To span a table cell across multiple columns, place two or more column separators (
) next to each other after the cell’s content. If the spanning cell is the last or only element of a row, you need to add the extra trailing pipe (
) for that row, otherwise DocC interprets the row as having an additional empty cell at the end. For example:
You might find it easier to discern the structure of your table from its markup if you use trailing pipes consistently when spanning cells.
A spanning cell determines its horizontal alignment from the left-most column that it spans. Going from left to right in the example below:
Cells “One” and “Five” use leading alignment because they both span the first column
Cell “Four” uses center alignment because it spans the second column
Cell “Two” uses trailing alignment because it spans the third column
To span a table cell across multiple rows, write the cell’s content in the first row and a single caret (
) in one or more cells below it. For example:
A table cell can span both columns and rows by combining the syntax for both. For example:

abstract.text: Produce rich API reference documentation and interactive tutorials for your Swift framework or package.
Elevate your content’s visual appeal by adding images.
Make symbols easier to find by arranging them into groups and collections.
